questions.question: Tawheed in Islam means:

options.B: the oneness and uniqueness of Allah in all aspects
options.C: the performance of Salah five times daily

questions.explanation: Tawheed is the fundamental Islamic concept of the absolute oneness of Allah — that He alone is worthy of worship and has no partners or equals.

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct meaning of 'La ilaha illa Allah'?

options.A: Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah
options.B: There is no god worthy of worship except Allah
options.D: All praise is due to Allah

questions.explanation: 'La ilaha illa Allah' means 'There is no deity worthy of worship except Allah.' It is the first part of the Shahadah (declaration of faith).

questions.question: Shirk in Islam refers to:

options.B: associating partners with Allah in worship

questions.explanation: Shirk is the unforgivable sin of associating partners with Allah or attributing divine qualities to others. It is the opposite of Tawheed.

questions.question: The three categories of Tawheed are Tawheed al-Rububiyyah, Tawheed al-Uluhiyyah, and:
questions.explanation: The three categories of Tawheed are: (1) Tawheed al-Rububiyyah (oneness of Lordship), (2) Tawheed al-Uluhiyyah (oneness of worship), and (3) Tawheed al-Asma wa al-Sifat (oneness of Allah's names and attributes).

questions.question: Which Surah of the Quran is considered the purest expression of Tawheed?
questions.explanation: Surah Al-Ikhlas (Chapter 112) is the epitome of Tawheed: 'Say: He is Allah, the One; Allah, the Eternal; He begets not, nor is He begotten; and there is none like Him.'

questions.question: The five pillars of Islam are Shahadah, Salah, Zakah, Sawm, and:
questions.explanation: The five pillars of Islam are: (1) Shahadah (declaration of faith), (2) Salah (prayer), (3) Zakah (almsgiving), (4) Sawm (fasting), and (5) Hajj (pilgrimage to Makkah).

questions.question: Zakah is obligatory on Muslims who possess wealth above the Nisab (minimum threshold) for a period of:
questions.explanation: Zakah becomes obligatory when a Muslim's wealth exceeds the Nisab and has been in their possession for a complete lunar year (Hawl). The standard rate is 2.5% of savings.

questions.question: The Hajj pilgrimage is obligatory for every Muslim who is physically and financially able, at least:
questions.explanation: Hajj is obligatory once in a lifetime for every adult Muslim who is physically capable and financially able to undertake the journey.

questions.question: The fast of Ramadan is observed during which month of the Islamic calendar?
questions.explanation: Sawm (fasting) is observed during Ramadan, the 9th month of the Islamic lunar calendar. Muslims fast from Fajr (dawn) to Maghrib (sunset) throughout the month.

questions.question: The Salah (prayer) that is performed at sunrise is called:
questions.explanation: Fajr is the dawn/morning prayer performed before sunrise. The five daily prayers are: Fajr (dawn), Dhuhr (midday), Asr (afternoon), Maghrib (sunset), Isha (night).

questions.question: The night prayer performed after Isha is called:
questions.explanation: Tahajjud is the voluntary night prayer performed after sleeping, before Fajr. It is highly recommended and was regularly practised by the Prophet Muhammad (SAW).

questions.question: The Zakah rate on gold, silver, and trade goods is:
questions.explanation: The Zakah rate on gold, silver, cash, and trade goods is 2.5% of the total amount that has been in one's possession for a full lunar year above the Nisab.

questions.question: The six articles of faith in Islam include belief in Allah, angels, books, messengers, the Last Day, and:
questions.explanation: The six articles of faith (Arkan al-Iman) are: (1) Allah, (2) Angels, (3) Books, (4) Messengers/Prophets, (5) The Last Day, and (6) Divine Decree (Qadar — both good and bad).

questions.question: The belief that Allah has predetermined all things in creation is called:
questions.explanation: Qadar (divine decree/predestination) is the belief that Allah knows and has ordained all things that happen in creation. Muslims believe in both divine will and human responsibility.

questions.question: The four revealed books mentioned in the Quran include the Torah, the Zabur, the Injeel, and:
questions.explanation: The four major revealed scriptures are: the Torah (given to Musa/Moses), Zabur/Psalms (Dawud/David), Injeel/Gospel (Isa/Jesus), and the Quran (Muhammad SAW). Muslims believe all were originally from Allah.

questions.question: Angels in Islam are created from:
questions.explanation: According to Islamic belief, angels are created from light (Nur). Humans are created from clay, and Jinn from smokeless fire.

questions.question: The angel responsible for delivering divine revelation to the prophets is:
questions.explanation: Jibril (Gabriel) is the angel of revelation who delivered Allah's messages to all prophets, including bringing the Quran to Prophet Muhammad (SAW).

questions.question: The Islamic belief in the Day of Judgment involves all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Islam does not teach reincarnation. On the Day of Judgment, people will be resurrected, their deeds weighed on the Mizan (scales), and they will cross the Sirat to reach their final abode.

questions.question: The Quran was revealed to Prophet Muhammad (SAW) over a period of approximately:
questions.explanation: The Quran was revealed over approximately 23 years: the first 13 years in Makkah (Makkan Surahs) and the next 10 years in Madinah (Madinan Surahs).

questions.question: The first Surah (chapter) of the Quran is:
questions.explanation: Surah Al-Fatiha (The Opening) is the first chapter of the Quran. It is recited in every unit (Rakah) of the daily prayers.

questions.question: The longest Surah in the Quran is:
questions.explanation: Surah Al-Baqarah (The Cow, Chapter 2) is the longest Surah in the Quran, containing 286 verses.

questions.question: The first verse (ayah) revealed to Prophet Muhammad (SAW) was from Surah:
questions.explanation: The first revelation was the opening verses of Surah Al-Alaq (96:1-5): 'Read in the name of your Lord who created...' This occurred in the Cave of Hira in 610 CE.

questions.question: The Quran is divided into how many Juz (parts)?
questions.explanation: The Quran is divided into 30 equal parts (Juz, plural Ajzaa) to facilitate reading it completely over a month (one Juz per day during Ramadan).

questions.question: The Quran has how many Surahs (chapters)?
questions.explanation: The Quran consists of 114 Surahs (chapters) and approximately 6,236 Ayat (verses).

questions.question: The compilation of the Quran into a single book (Mushaf) was ordered by Caliph:
questions.explanation: Abu Bakr Al-Siddiq (the first Caliph) ordered the compilation of the Quran into a single book after the Battle of Yamama (632 CE), where many Quran memorizers (Huffaz) were killed.

questions.question: The standardization and distribution of the Quran in a single dialect (Quraysh dialect) was carried out by Caliph:
questions.explanation: Caliph Uthman ibn Affan standardized the Quran into a single official copy (the Uthmanic Mushaf) in the Quraysh dialect and distributed copies to different regions to prevent disagreements.

questions.question: The study of the circumstances of the revelation of Quranic verses is called:
questions.explanation: Asbab al-Nuzul (causes/occasions of revelation) is the study of the historical contexts and events that prompted the revelation of specific Quranic verses.

questions.question: Tafsir refers to:

options.A: recitation of the Quran with proper rules
options.B: interpretation and explanation of the meaning of the Quran

questions.explanation: Tafsir is the scholarly interpretation and explanation of the Quran's meanings, context, and application. Famous Tafsir works include those of Ibn Kathir and Al-Tabari.

questions.question: Tajweed refers to:

options.A: the art of reciting the Quran with proper pronunciation, rhythm, and rules

questions.explanation: Tajweed is the set of rules governing the correct pronunciation and recitation of the Quran, preserving the original sounds as revealed to Prophet Muhammad (SAW).

questions.question: A person who has memorized the entire Quran is called a:
questions.explanation: A Hafiz (feminine: Hafiza) is a Muslim who has memorized the entire Quran (all 114 Surahs). This is considered a great honour in Islamic tradition.

questions.question: The Sunnah refers to:

options.B: the sayings, actions, and silent approvals of Prophet Muhammad (SAW)

questions.explanation: The Sunnah encompasses the Prophet's (SAW) words (Hadith Qawli), actions (Hadith Fi'li), and silent approvals (Hadith Taqriri). It is the second source of Islamic law after the Quran.

questions.question: A Hadith is classified as 'Sahih' (authentic) when:

options.A: it is found in any book
options.B: it has a continuous chain of trustworthy narrators back to the Prophet (SAW)
options.C: it is approved by a Mufti
options.D: it agrees with the Quran only

questions.explanation: A Sahih (authentic) Hadith has a continuous (Muttasil) chain of trustworthy (Thiqah) narrators, the text is sound, and it is free from irregularities and defects.

questions.question: The compiler of the most authentic Hadith collection, Sahih al-Bukhari, was:
questions.explanation: Imam Muhammad ibn Ismail al-Bukhari (810-870 CE) compiled Sahih al-Bukhari, considered the most authentic Hadith collection after the Quran by Sunni Muslims.

questions.question: The six major Hadith collections in Sunni Islam are collectively called:
questions.explanation: Al-Kutub al-Sitta (The Six Books) are: Sahih al-Bukhari, Sahih Muslim, Sunan Abu Dawud, Jami al-Tirmidhi, Sunan al-Nasa'i, and Sunan Ibn Majah.

questions.question: A Hadith Qudsi is:

options.B: a divine Hadith where Allah speaks in the first person through the Prophet
options.D: a Hadith with a weak chain of narration

questions.explanation: A Hadith Qudsi (sacred/holy Hadith) is a Hadith in which the Prophet (SAW) reports words from Allah in the first person, distinct from both the Quran and ordinary Hadith.

questions.question: The chain of narrators (transmission chain) of a Hadith is called:
questions.explanation: The Isnad (Sanad) is the chain of narrators through whom a Hadith was transmitted. The Matn is the text (content) of the Hadith. Hadith criticism evaluates the reliability of the Isnad.

questions.question: The four main sources of Islamic law (Sharia) are:
questions.explanation: The four primary sources of Islamic jurisprudence (Usul al-Fiqh) are: (1) Quran, (2) Sunnah, (3) Ijma (consensus of scholars), and (4) Qiyas (analogical reasoning).

questions.question: Ijma in Islamic law refers to:

options.B: the consensus of Islamic scholars on a legal matter

questions.explanation: Ijma is the consensus of qualified Islamic scholars (Mujtahidun) of a particular era on a legal ruling. It is the third source of Islamic law.

questions.question: Qiyas in Islamic law refers to:

options.B: analogical deduction — applying a ruling from an established case to a new similar case

questions.explanation: Qiyas is analogical reasoning — deriving legal rulings for new situations by analogy with established rulings from the Quran or Sunnah when they share the same underlying cause (Illah).

questions.question: The four main schools of Islamic jurisprudence (Madhabs) in Sunni Islam are:
questions.explanation: The four Sunni Madhabs (schools of jurisprudence) are: Hanafi (Imam Abu Hanifa), Maliki (Imam Malik), Shafi'i (Imam Al-Shafi'i), and Hanbali (Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal).

questions.question: The five categories of Islamic legal rulings (Ahkam al-Khamsa) are Wajib, Mandub, Mubah, Makruh, and:
questions.explanation: The five categories of Islamic law are: Wajib (obligatory), Mandub (recommended), Mubah (permissible/neutral), Makruh (disliked), and Haram (forbidden/prohibited).

questions.question: Ijtihad is defined as:

options.B: independent scholarly reasoning to derive Islamic rulings

questions.explanation: Ijtihad is the process of independent scholarly effort and reasoning by a qualified scholar (Mujtahid) to derive new legal rulings for issues not explicitly addressed in the Quran or Sunnah.

questions.question: Taqlid means:

options.B: following the legal opinion of a scholar or Madhab without necessarily knowing the evidence

questions.explanation: Taqlid is the practice of following the legal rulings of a qualified scholar or Madhab, accepting their conclusions without demanding proof. It is the opposite of Ijtihad.

questions.question: Prophet Muhammad (SAW) was born in:
questions.explanation: Prophet Muhammad (SAW) was born in Makkah (in present-day Saudi Arabia) in approximately 570 CE, in the Year of the Elephant.

questions.question: The migration of Prophet Muhammad (SAW) from Makkah to Madinah is called:
questions.explanation: The Hijrah (migration) in 622 CE marks the Prophet's (SAW) migration from Makkah to Madinah. It is so significant that the Islamic calendar (Hijri) begins from this event.

questions.question: Prophet Muhammad (SAW) received his first revelation at the age of:
questions.explanation: Prophet Muhammad (SAW) received his first revelation in the Cave of Hira at the age of 40, in 610 CE.

questions.question: The wife of Prophet Muhammad (SAW) who was present during his first revelation was:
questions.explanation: Khadijah bint Khuwaylid (RA) was the Prophet's first wife and the first person to accept Islam. She comforted the Prophet (SAW) after his first revelation and supported him throughout his mission.

questions.question: The first man to accept Islam was:
questions.explanation: Abu Bakr Al-Siddiq (RA) is widely regarded as the first adult free man to accept Islam. Ali ibn Abi Talib (RA) was the first child, and Khadijah (RA) was the first overall.

questions.question: The Battle of Badr (624 CE) was significant because it was:

options.A: the first major battle in which Muslims were defeated
options.B: the first major military victory of the Muslims against the Quraysh
options.C: the battle where the Prophet (SAW) was injured

questions.explanation: The Battle of Badr (2 AH/624 CE) was the first major battle between the Muslims and the Quraysh of Makkah. The Muslims (313) decisively defeated the Quraysh (1,000+), establishing Islam's military strength.

questions.question: The Prophet (SAW) was injured in which battle?
questions.explanation: The Prophet (SAW) was injured in the Battle of Uhud (3 AH/625 CE) when Muslim archers disobeyed his orders, allowing the Quraysh cavalry to attack from the rear.

questions.question: The conquest of Makkah took place in:
questions.explanation: The Conquest of Makkah (Fath Makkah) occurred in 8 AH (630 CE), when the Prophet (SAW) led an army of 10,000 Muslims and entered Makkah with minimal resistance.

questions.question: The Prophet Muhammad (SAW) passed away in:
questions.explanation: Prophet Muhammad (SAW) passed away on 12 Rabi al-Awwal, 11 AH (June 8, 632 CE) in Madinah, at the age of approximately 63 years.

questions.question: The Night Journey and Ascension of Prophet Muhammad (SAW) is referred to as:
questions.explanation: Isra wal Miraj refers to the miraculous night journey from Makkah to Jerusalem (Isra) and the ascent through the heavens to Allah's presence (Miraj), during which the five daily prayers were ordained.

questions.question: The treaty signed between the Muslims and the Quraysh of Makkah in 628 CE was called:
questions.explanation: The Treaty of Hudaybiyyah (6 AH/628 CE) was a 10-year peace agreement between the Muslims and Makkans. Though apparently unfavorable, it allowed Islam to spread and led eventually to the Conquest of Makkah.

questions.question: The Farewell Pilgrimage (Hajjat al-Wada) was performed by the Prophet (SAW) in:
questions.explanation: The Prophet (SAW) performed his only Hajj, the Farewell Pilgrimage (Hajjat al-Wada), in 10 AH (632 CE). During this pilgrimage, he delivered the famous Farewell Sermon.

questions.topic: Islamic History - The Rightly Guided Caliphs
questions.question: The first Caliph after the death of Prophet Muhammad (SAW) was:
questions.explanation: Abu Bakr Al-Siddiq (RA) was the first Caliph (632-634 CE). He suppressed the Ridda (apostasy) wars, consolidated Islamic unity, and began the compilation of the Quran.

questions.topic: Islamic History - The Rightly Guided Caliphs
questions.question: The Caliph who expanded the Islamic state most rapidly and introduced systematic governance (Diwan) was:
questions.explanation: Umar ibn al-Khattab (RA), the second Caliph (634-644 CE), expanded Islam into Persia, Egypt, and Syria, established the Islamic calendar, created the Diwan (administrative register), and introduced systematic governance.

questions.topic: Islamic History - The Rightly Guided Caliphs
questions.question: The Caliph who standardized the Quran into one official text was:
questions.explanation: Uthman ibn Affan (RA), the third Caliph (644-656 CE), standardized the Quran into a single official copy (Uthmanic Mushaf) to prevent variant readings from spreading.

questions.topic: Islamic History - The Rightly Guided Caliphs
questions.question: The period of the four Rightly Guided Caliphs (Khulafa al-Rashidun) lasted from:
questions.explanation: The Khulafa al-Rashidun (Rightly Guided Caliphs) period lasted from 632 CE (death of the Prophet SAW) to 661 CE (assassination of Ali RA), covering the reigns of Abu Bakr, Umar, Uthman, and Ali.

questions.topic: Islamic History - The Rightly Guided Caliphs
questions.question: The fourth Caliph and cousin/son-in-law of Prophet Muhammad (SAW) was:
questions.explanation: Ali ibn Abi Talib (RA) was the fourth Caliph (656-661 CE). He was both the cousin and son-in-law of the Prophet (SAW), married to the Prophet's daughter Fatimah (RA).

questions.question: The Umayyad Caliphate was founded by:
questions.explanation: Muawiyah ibn Abi Sufyan founded the Umayyad Caliphate in 661 CE, moving the capital from Kufa to Damascus. The Umayyad dynasty ruled until 750 CE.

questions.question: The Abbasid Caliphate was known as the golden age of Islamic civilization. Its capital was:
questions.explanation: The Abbasid Caliphate (750-1258 CE) established its capital at Baghdad (present-day Iraq), which became the greatest city of the medieval world and center of Islamic learning.

questions.question: The Abbasid Caliphate ended with the Mongol destruction of Baghdad in:
questions.explanation: The Mongols under Hulagu Khan destroyed Baghdad in 1258 CE, killing the last Abbasid Caliph Al-Musta'sim and ending the Abbasid Caliphate in Baghdad.

questions.question: Islam was introduced to West Africa primarily through:

options.B: Arab and Berber trans-Saharan traders and scholars

questions.explanation: Islam spread to West Africa mainly through trans-Saharan trade routes, with Arab and Berber traders and Muslim scholars bringing the faith to trading centers like Timbuktu, Kano, and Kanem-Bornu.

questions.question: The Sokoto Caliphate was founded by Usman Dan Fodio in:
questions.explanation: Usman Dan Fodio launched the Sokoto Jihad in 1804, establishing the Sokoto Caliphate in present-day Nigeria by 1809. It became one of the largest states in 19th-century Africa.

questions.question: The Sokoto Jihad led by Usman Dan Fodio was primarily aimed at:

options.B: purifying Islam from syncretic practices and establishing an Islamic state
options.C: expanding trade routes across the Sahara

questions.explanation: Usman Dan Fodio's Jihad aimed to purify Islam in the Hausa states from syncretic and un-Islamic practices and establish governance based on Sharia law.

questions.question: The famous West African Muslim scholar and traveler who visited Mali in the 14th century was:
questions.explanation: Ibn Battuta, the famous Moroccan explorer, visited the Mali Empire in 1352-1353 CE and left detailed accounts of Islamic life in West Africa in his travel memoir, the Rihla.

questions.question: The Malian king who made the famous pilgrimage to Makkah in 1324-1325 CE, distributing gold freely, was:
questions.explanation: Mansa Musa, the emperor of the Mali Empire, made his famous Hajj pilgrimage to Makkah in 1324-1325 CE with a large entourage and distributed so much gold that he caused inflation in Egypt.

questions.question: The city that became the most important center of Islamic scholarship in West Africa was:
questions.explanation: Timbuktu (in present-day Mali) became the greatest center of Islamic learning in West Africa, housing Sankore University and thousands of scholars and manuscripts.

questions.question: The concept of Akhlaq in Islam refers to:
questions.explanation: Akhlaq (plural of Khuluq) refers to Islamic ethics, moral character, and virtuous behavior. The Prophet (SAW) said: 'I was sent to perfect good moral character.'

questions.question: Honesty and truthfulness in Islam is expressed by the Arabic term:
questions.explanation: Sidq (truthfulness/honesty) is a fundamental Islamic virtue. The Prophet (SAW) was known as 'Al-Sadiq' (the truthful) even before prophethood.

questions.question: Amanah in Islam refers to:
questions.explanation: Amanah means trustworthiness, fulfilling promises, and discharging entrusted responsibilities faithfully. It is a core Islamic virtue applied in all areas of life.

questions.question: Taqwa in Islam means:
questions.explanation: Taqwa is the Islamic concept of God-consciousness, piety, and fearing Allah. It involves being aware of Allah in all actions and avoiding what is displeasing to Him.

questions.question: The concept of Sabr in Islam means:

options.B: patience and steadfastness in the face of trials

questions.explanation: Sabr is the Islamic virtue of patience, perseverance, and steadfastness when facing difficulties, hardships, or temptations. It is mentioned numerous times in the Quran.

questions.question: Shukr in Islam means:
questions.explanation: Shukr means gratitude and thankfulness to Allah for His blessings. The Quran states: 'If you are grateful, I will surely increase you in favor' (14:7).

questions.question: Tawbah (repentance) in Islam requires:

options.B: sincere regret, stopping the sin, resolving not to repeat it, and making amends if rights were violated

questions.explanation: Valid Tawbah requires: (1) genuine remorse for the sin, (2) ceasing the sin immediately, (3) firm resolve not to return to it, and (4) restoring the rights of others if violated.

questions.question: Tawadu (humility) in Islam means:
questions.explanation: Tawadu is humility — being free from arrogance and pride, acknowledging one's dependence on Allah and treating others respectfully regardless of status.

questions.question: Wudu (ablution) is a prerequisite for which of the following acts of worship?
questions.explanation: Wudu (ritual ablution) is required before performing Salah (prayer) and before touching or carrying the Quran. It involves washing specific parts of the body in the prescribed manner.

questions.question: Ghusl (full ritual bath) is required in Islam after:

options.B: sexual intercourse, ejaculation, or the end of menstruation

questions.explanation: Ghusl (full body ritual purification) is obligatory after sexual intercourse, ejaculation, the completion of menstruation (Haid), and post-natal bleeding (Nifas).

questions.question: The Adhan is the Islamic call to:
questions.explanation: The Adhan (call to prayer) is the verbal announcement that it is time for Salah. It is called from the minaret of the mosque by the Muadhdhin (caller to prayer).

questions.question: The direction Muslims face during Salah (prayer) is towards:
questions.explanation: Muslims face the Kaabah (the sacred house in Makkah) during Salah. This direction is called the Qiblah. Initially Muslims prayed towards Jerusalem before the Qiblah was changed.

questions.question: The Friday congregational prayer (Jumu'ah) replaces which daily prayer?
questions.explanation: The Friday Jumu'ah prayer replaces the midday Dhuhr prayer for adult male Muslims who are resident and healthy. It includes a Khutbah (sermon) delivered by the Imam.

questions.question: The prayer performed for the deceased Muslim before burial is called:
questions.explanation: Salat al-Janazah is the funeral prayer performed for a deceased Muslim as a communal obligation (Fard Kifayah). It consists of four Takbirat without bowing or prostration.

questions.question: The Night of Power (Laylat al-Qadr), better than a thousand months, falls in which period?
questions.explanation: Laylat al-Qadr (the Night of Power) falls in one of the odd-numbered nights of the last ten nights of Ramadan. Muslims are encouraged to increase worship during this period.

questions.question: Eid al-Adha is celebrated on the:
questions.explanation: Eid al-Adha (Festival of Sacrifice) is celebrated on the 10th of Dhul Hijjah (the month of Hajj), commemorating Prophet Ibrahim's (AS) willingness to sacrifice his son Ismail (AS).

questions.question: Eid al-Fitr is celebrated on the:
questions.explanation: Eid al-Fitr (Festival of Breaking the Fast) is celebrated on the 1st of Shawwal, the first day after Ramadan ends, to mark the successful completion of a month of fasting.

questions.question: Itikaf is an act of worship that involves:

options.A: voluntary fasting on Mondays and Thursdays
options.B: seclusion in the mosque for worship, especially in the last ten days of Ramadan

questions.explanation: Itikaf is the Sunnah practice of staying in seclusion in the mosque, especially during the last ten days of Ramadan, devoting oneself entirely to worship and seeking Laylat al-Qadr.

questions.question: The state of ritual purity and consecration entered upon starting Hajj or Umrah is called:
questions.explanation: Ihram is the sacred state of ritual purity assumed for Hajj or Umrah, marked by wearing two plain white seamless garments (for men) and following specific prohibitions.

questions.question: Tawaf during Hajj refers to:

options.B: the ritual circling of the Kaabah seven times counter-clockwise
options.C: standing on the plain of Arafat

questions.explanation: Tawaf is the ritual of circumambulating (walking around) the Kaabah seven times in a counter-clockwise direction, beginning and ending at the Black Stone (Hajar al-Aswad).

questions.question: The Sa'i ritual during Hajj commemorates the action of:

options.B: Hajar (Hagar) running between Safa and Marwa in search of water for her son Ismail
options.D: Adam and Hawwa meeting on Mount Arafat

questions.explanation: Sa'i (walking seven times between Safa and Marwa) commemorates Hajar's (Hagar's) desperate search for water for baby Ismail. The spring of Zamzam miraculously appeared as a result.

questions.question: The most important pillar of Hajj, without which the Hajj is invalid, is:

options.B: Wuquf at Arafat (standing on the plain of Arafat)

questions.explanation: Wuquf (standing) at the plain of Arafat on the 9th of Dhul Hijjah is the essential pillar of Hajj. The Prophet (SAW) said: 'Hajj is Arafat.' Missing it invalidates the Hajj.

questions.question: The stoning of the Jamarat during Hajj symbolizes:
questions.explanation: The stoning of the three pillars (Jamarat) symbolizes the rejection of Satan's whispers, as Ibrahim (AS) threw stones at the devil when he tried to dissuade Ibrahim from obeying Allah.

questions.question: In Islamic marriage (Nikah), the Mahr (dowry) is:

options.A: a gift from the bride to the groom
options.B: a mandatory gift from the groom to the bride
options.C: a payment from the bride's family to the groom

questions.explanation: The Mahr is a mandatory gift or sum of money that the groom must pay to the bride as part of the marriage contract. It is her exclusive right and property.

questions.question: The conditions for a valid Islamic marriage (Nikah) include all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Essential conditions for valid Nikah are: offer (Ijab) and acceptance (Qabul), Mahr, two competent Muslim witnesses, and the bride's guardian (Wali). Government approval is not an Islamic requirement.

questions.question: Polygamy in Islam allows a Muslim man to marry up to a maximum of:
questions.explanation: The Quran (4:3) allows a man to marry up to four wives, but only on the strict condition of treating them all with absolute justice and equity. If he cannot do so, he must marry only one.

questions.question: Talaq in Islamic law refers to:
questions.explanation: Talaq is the Islamic form of divorce initiated by the husband by pronouncing the formula of divorce. It may be revocable (Talaq Raj'i) or irrevocable (Talaq Ba'in).

questions.question: Khul' in Islamic law is:

options.B: divorce initiated by the wife by returning the Mahr
options.D: the period of waiting after divorce

questions.explanation: Khul' is a form of divorce where the wife initiates separation by returning the Mahr (or agreed compensation) to the husband. It is her right under Islamic law.

questions.question: The waiting period (Iddah) after divorce for a woman who is pregnant is:
questions.explanation: For a pregnant woman, the Iddah (waiting period) after divorce or death of husband is until she gives birth (Quran 65:4). For a non-pregnant woman, it is three menstrual cycles.

questions.question: Islamic inheritance law (Mirath/Fara'id) distributes an estate primarily based on:

options.B: Quranic prescriptions of fixed shares (Fard) for specific heirs

questions.explanation: Islamic inheritance law (Fara'id/Ilm al-Mirath) is based on Quranic verses (4:11-12, 4:176) that specify fixed shares (Fard) for designated heirs like spouse, children, and parents.

questions.question: In Islamic inheritance, a daughter receives what fraction of a son's share?

options.C: Half the share of the son

questions.explanation: Quran 4:11 states that a son receives twice the share of a daughter. This is because a son has financial obligations (Nafaqah) to support his wife and family, while a woman's inheritance is her own.

questions.question: The first prophet in Islam is believed to be:
questions.explanation: Adam (AS) is considered the first human being and the first prophet in Islamic belief. He was created by Allah and taught the names of all things.

questions.question: The prophet known as 'Khalilullah' (Friend of Allah) is:
questions.explanation: Ibrahim (Abraham, AS) is given the title 'Khalilullah' (Friend of Allah) because of his complete submission to Allah and absolute trust in Him.

questions.question: The prophet known as 'Kalimullah' (One who spoke directly to Allah) is:
questions.explanation: Musa (Moses, AS) is called 'Kalimullah' because Allah spoke to him directly without an intermediary, as described in Surah Al-Nisa (4:164) and other places in the Quran.

questions.question: The prophet known as 'Ruhullah' (Spirit of Allah) is:
questions.explanation: Isa (Jesus, AS) is referred to as 'Ruhullah' (a Spirit from Allah) and 'Kalimatullah' (Word of Allah) in the Quran, referring to his miraculous virgin birth.

questions.question: The prophet who was swallowed by a whale is:
questions.explanation: Prophet Yunus (Jonah, AS), also known as Dhul-Nun (owner of the whale), was swallowed by a large fish after leaving his mission without permission. He prayed to Allah from within the whale.

questions.question: The prophet known for his extreme patience during prolonged illness is:
questions.explanation: Prophet Ayyub (Job, AS) is renowned for his extraordinary patience (Sabr) during a prolonged period of illness and suffering. He is the exemplar of patience in Islamic tradition.

questions.question: The prophet sent to the people of 'Ad was:
questions.explanation: Prophet Hud (AS) was sent to the people of 'Ad, a powerful civilization in ancient Arabia. They rejected him and were destroyed by a violent windstorm (Quran 7:65-72).

questions.question: The prophet sent to the people of Thamud was:
questions.explanation: Prophet Salih (AS) was sent to the Thamud people. He was given a she-camel as a miracle. When they killed the camel, they were destroyed by a thunderous blast.

questions.question: The total number of prophets mentioned by name in the Quran is:
questions.explanation: The Quran mentions 25 prophets by name. Islamic tradition holds that Allah sent 124,000 prophets throughout history, but only 25 are named in the Quran.

questions.question: Prophet Muhammad (SAW) is described in the Quran as:

options.B: the seal (last) of the prophets (Khatam al-Nabiyyin)
options.C: the prophet only to the Arabs

questions.explanation: The Quran (33:40) describes Prophet Muhammad (SAW) as 'Khatam al-Nabiyyin' (the Seal of the Prophets), meaning he is the last and final prophet — no prophet will come after him.

questions.question: The Islamic calendar (Hijri calendar) is a:
questions.explanation: The Islamic Hijri calendar is a purely lunar calendar, with each month starting with the sighting of the new crescent moon. The lunar year is approximately 354 days (11 days shorter than the solar year).

questions.question: The Islamic Hijri calendar starts from the year of the Prophet's (SAW):
questions.explanation: The Hijri calendar begins from the year of the Prophet's (SAW) migration (Hijrah) from Makkah to Madinah in 622 CE, as established by the second Caliph Umar ibn al-Khattab (RA).

questions.question: The first month of the Islamic calendar is:
questions.explanation: Muharram is the first month of the Islamic lunar calendar. It is one of the four sacred months (along with Rajab, Dhul Qa'dah, and Dhul Hijjah).

questions.question: The Day of Ashura falls on which date of Muharram?
questions.explanation: Ashura is on the 10th of Muharram. It is the day Allah saved Musa (Moses, AS) and the Children of Israel from Pharaoh. Fasting on this day is recommended (Sunnah).

questions.question: The month in which the Prophet Muhammad (SAW) is traditionally believed to have been born is:
questions.explanation: Prophet Muhammad (SAW) is traditionally believed to have been born on the 12th of Rabi al-Awwal (the third month of the Islamic calendar), the event commemorated as Mawlid al-Nabi.

questions.question: Riba (usury/interest) in Islam refers to:

options.B: the prohibited practice of charging or paying interest on loans

questions.explanation: Riba is the Arabic term for any excess or unjust increase in a loan transaction (interest). The Quran strictly prohibits Riba (2:275-279) as it is considered exploitative.

questions.question: Waqf in Islamic economics is:

options.B: a voluntary charitable endowment of property for a religious or charitable purpose
options.D: tax paid to the Islamic state

questions.explanation: Waqf is an Islamic endowment — voluntary dedication of property (buildings, land, or assets) for perpetual charitable or religious use. Its benefits are given to the community while the property is preserved.

questions.question: Murabaha in Islamic banking is:

options.B: a cost-plus-profit sale where the bank buys and resells an asset at a disclosed profit margin

questions.explanation: Murabaha is an Islamic finance arrangement where a bank purchases an asset and resells it to the client at a higher price (cost + agreed profit), paid in installments. No interest is charged.

questions.question: Musharakah in Islamic finance refers to:

options.B: a partnership where all partners contribute capital and share profits and losses

questions.explanation: Musharakah (partnership) is an Islamic finance contract where two or more parties contribute capital to a business venture and share profits and losses according to agreed ratios.

questions.question: Sadaqah differs from Zakah in that Sadaqah is:

options.B: voluntary charity given at any time with no fixed amount

questions.explanation: Sadaqah is voluntary charity given at any time, for any amount, to any worthy cause. Zakah is the obligatory annual charity with specific rules on amount, eligibility, and recipients.

questions.question: The greater Jihad (Al-Jihad al-Akbar) refers to:

options.A: armed conflict against enemies of Islam
options.B: the internal struggle against one's own desires and ego

questions.explanation: The greater Jihad is the internal, spiritual struggle against one's own nafs (ego, desires, and sinful inclinations). The Prophet (SAW) described it as greater than physical warfare.

questions.question: The lesser Jihad (Al-Jihad al-Asghar) refers to:

options.B: armed struggle in defence of Islam under specific conditions

questions.explanation: The lesser Jihad refers to armed struggle (Qital) in the path of Allah under strict conditions and rules. Islam governs warfare extensively through Islamic laws of Jihad.

questions.question: Surah Al-Fatiha is also called 'Umm al-Quran' meaning:
questions.explanation: Surah Al-Fatiha is called 'Umm al-Quran' (Mother of the Quran) because it encapsulates the essential themes of the entire Quran: praise of Allah, His attributes, guidance, and the Day of Judgment.

questions.question: Ayat al-Kursi (Verse of the Throne) is found in Surah:
questions.explanation: Ayat al-Kursi (2:255) is found in Surah Al-Baqarah. It is considered the greatest verse in the Quran and describes Allah's attributes of all-knowingness and sovereignty over creation.

questions.question: The two Surahs of seeking refuge (Mu'awwidhatayn) are:
questions.explanation: The Mu'awwidhatayn are Surah Al-Falaq (113) and Surah Al-Nas (114), the last two Surahs of the Quran, revealed for protection from evil, black magic, and Satan's whispers.

questions.question: Surah Yasin is often called the 'heart of the Quran' because it:

options.B: deals comprehensively with resurrection, divine power, and the fundamental themes of faith

questions.explanation: Surah Yasin (36) is called the 'heart of the Quran' (as mentioned in Hadith) because it deals with core Quranic themes: resurrection, the power of Allah, prophethood, and faith.

questions.question: Surah Al-Kahf contains the story of which group of young men who sought refuge in a cave?

options.B: The People of the Cave (Ashab al-Kahf)

questions.explanation: Surah Al-Kahf (Chapter 18) tells the story of the People of the Cave (Ashab al-Kahf) — pious young men who fled from a polytheistic society and were put to sleep by Allah for 309 years.

questions.question: Tayammum is the dry ablution using clean earth and is permitted when:

options.A: one is too tired to perform Wudu
options.B: water is unavailable or its use would cause harm to one's health

questions.explanation: Tayammum (dry purification with clean earth/dust) is a concession allowed when water is unavailable or when using water would be harmful to one's health. It temporarily replaces Wudu and Ghusl.

questions.question: The concept of Najasah in Islam refers to:

options.B: ritual impurity — substances that are considered physically and ritually unclean

questions.explanation: Najasah refers to ritually impure substances (like blood, urine, feces, pork, alcohol). Removing Najasah is required for prayer and proper Islamic practice.

questions.question: Istinja in Islam refers to:

options.B: cleaning oneself after answering the call of nature

questions.explanation: Istinja is the practice of cleaning oneself after using the toilet — using water and/or permissible materials to remove impurities from the private parts. It is obligatory in Islam.

questions.question: The Companions of the Prophet (SAW) are called:
questions.explanation: The Sahabah (singular: Sahabi) are the companions — Muslims who personally met Prophet Muhammad (SAW), believed in him, and died as Muslims. They are highly revered in Islamic tradition.

questions.question: The ten Companions given glad tidings of Paradise (Al-Ashratu al-Mubashsharun) include all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: The ten blessed Companions (Al-Ashratu al-Mubashsharun) are: Abu Bakr, Umar, Uthman, Ali, Talhah, Zubayr, Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf, Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas, Sa'id ibn Zayd, and Abu Ubayda. Abdullah ibn Masud is not in this list.

questions.question: The companion known as 'Sayf Allah al-Maslul' (the drawn sword of Allah) was:
questions.explanation: Khalid ibn al-Walid (RA) was given the title 'Sayf Allah al-Maslul' (Drawn Sword of Allah) by the Prophet (SAW) for his extraordinary military victories and leadership.

questions.question: The companion known as 'The Lion of Allah' (Asadullah) was:
questions.explanation: Hamzah ibn Abd al-Muttalib (RA), the Prophet's uncle, was known as 'Asadullah' (Lion of Allah) and 'Sayyid al-Shuhada' (Master of Martyrs). He was martyred at the Battle of Uhud.

questions.question: The first Muadhdhin (caller to prayer) in Islam was:
questions.explanation: Bilal ibn Rabah (RA), a formerly enslaved Abyssinian Muslim, was chosen by the Prophet (SAW) as the first Muadhdhin (caller to prayer), renowned for his powerful voice and steadfast faith.

questions.question: The importance of seeking knowledge in Islam is emphasized by the Prophet's (SAW) saying:

options.B: 'Seeking knowledge is obligatory upon every Muslim'
options.C: 'The best of you is he who learns the Quran and teaches it'
options.D: 'Charity extinguishes sin as water extinguishes fire'

questions.explanation: The Prophet (SAW) said: 'Seeking knowledge is an obligation upon every Muslim.' This Hadith establishes that acquiring Islamic knowledge is a religious duty for all Muslims.

questions.question: The Dar al-Hikmah (House of Wisdom) established during the Abbasid period was famous as:

options.B: a great center of translation, learning, and scientific research

questions.explanation: The Bayt al-Hikmah (House of Wisdom) in Baghdad under the Abbasids (particularly Caliph Harun al-Rashid and Al-Ma'mun) was a renowned center of scholarship, translation, and scientific advancement.

questions.question: Imam Al-Ghazali's most famous work 'Ihya Ulum al-Din' (Revival of the Religious Sciences) deals with:

options.B: the revival of Islamic spiritual, moral, and practical religious life

questions.explanation: Imam Abu Hamid al-Ghazali's 'Ihya Ulum al-Din' is a comprehensive guide to Islamic spirituality, ethics, and practice, covering worship, social conduct, and spiritual development.

questions.question: Ibn Khaldun, the Muslim scholar who pioneered sociology and historiography, was from:
questions.explanation: Ibn Khaldun (1332-1406 CE), one of the greatest intellectuals in history, was born in Tunis (Tunisia). His Muqaddimah pioneered the philosophy of history and sociology.

questions.question: The Muslim physician and philosopher known as 'Shaykh al-Ra'is' (Prince of Physicians) who wrote 'Al-Qanun fi al-Tibb' was:
questions.explanation: Ibn Sina (Avicenna, 980-1037 CE) was a Persian polymath whose 'Al-Qanun fi al-Tibb' (Canon of Medicine) was the standard medical textbook in Europe and the Islamic world for centuries.

questions.question: The scholar who founded the Hanafi school of jurisprudence was:
questions.explanation: Imam Abu Hanifa al-Numan (699-767 CE) founded the Hanafi Madhab, one of the four major Sunni schools of jurisprudence. The Hanafi school is followed widely in Turkey, Central Asia, and South Asia.

questions.question: Imam Malik ibn Anas authored which famous legal compendium?
questions.explanation: Imam Malik ibn Anas compiled Al-Muwatta, one of the earliest collections of Hadith and Islamic law, which forms the basis of the Maliki Madhab (widely followed in North and West Africa).

questions.question: The Islamic principle of Shura means:

options.A: the absolute authority of the ruler
options.B: consultation and mutual advice in decision-making

questions.explanation: Shura (consultation) is the Islamic principle of seeking counsel from qualified people in decision-making. The Quran (42:38) mentions it as a key characteristic of the Muslim community.

questions.question: The Islamic concept of Ummah refers to:

options.B: the global community of Muslims united by faith

questions.explanation: The Ummah is the worldwide Muslim community — the brotherhood and sisterhood of all believers regardless of race, nationality, or language, united by Islam.

questions.question: The concept of Hisbah in Islamic governance refers to:

options.B: the institution for ensuring Islamic moral standards and market regulation

questions.explanation: Hisbah is the Islamic institution of moral accountability — ensuring that Islamic ethical standards are maintained in the community and markets, managed by the Muhtasib (market inspector).

questions.question: Halal food in Islam refers to food that is:

options.B: lawful and permissible according to Islamic law

questions.explanation: Halal (lawful) food must meet Islamic requirements: slaughtered in Allah's name, free from prohibited substances (pork, blood, alcohol), and prepared in a clean manner.

questions.question: Which of the following is clearly prohibited (Haram) in Islam?
questions.explanation: The Quran (2:173, 5:3) explicitly prohibits consuming pork (swine), blood, carrion (dead meat), and food dedicated to other than Allah. Beef, fish, and chicken are generally Halal.

questions.question: Dhabh (Islamic slaughter) requires that the animal be slaughtered by:

options.B: cutting the jugular vein, carotid arteries, and windpipe swiftly while invoking Allah's name

questions.explanation: Islamic Dhabh requires: a swift cut through the jugular vein, carotid arteries, and windpipe; invoking 'Bismillah, Allahu Akbar'; performed by a Muslim; the animal must be alive and healthy.

questions.question: Nifaq (hypocrisy) in Islam refers to:

options.B: outwardly professing Islam while inwardly disbelieving or concealing evil intentions

questions.explanation: Nifaq is hypocrisy — outwardly claiming faith while inwardly rejecting or conspiring against Islam. The Quran severely warns against the Munafiqun (hypocrites) in Surah Al-Munafiqun.

questions.question: Kufr in Islam means:

options.B: unbelief — rejection of Allah, the Prophet, or the fundamental tenets of Islam

questions.explanation: Kufr means disbelief or rejection — denying the existence of Allah, rejecting prophethood, or rejecting fundamental articles of faith. It is the opposite of Iman (faith).

questions.question: Iman (faith) in Islam is composed of:

options.B: belief in the heart, confession by the tongue, and action by the limbs
options.C: performance of the five pillars alone

questions.explanation: According to Islamic theology, Iman consists of three components: conviction in the heart (Tasdiq), verbal declaration by the tongue (Iqrar), and action by the limbs (Amal).

questions.question: The Islamic concept of Barzakh refers to:

options.B: the intermediate state between death and resurrection

questions.explanation: Barzakh is the intermediate realm between death and the Day of Resurrection. Souls remain in Barzakh (experiencing either bliss or punishment) until the Day of Judgment.

questions.question: The questioning in the grave by two angels (Munkar and Nakir) is about:
questions.explanation: According to Hadith, after burial the angels Munkar and Nakir ask the deceased: 'Who is your Lord? What is your religion? Who is your prophet?' The answers reveal the person's true faith.

questions.question: The Mizan (balance/scales) on the Day of Judgment is used to:

options.B: weigh the deeds of people — good deeds on one side and bad on the other

questions.explanation: The Mizan (scale) on the Day of Judgment will weigh each person's deeds. Those whose good deeds outweigh the bad will be rewarded; those whose bad deeds outweigh the good will face punishment.

questions.question: According to Islamic belief, the Sirat is:

options.B: a bridge over Hellfire that all people must cross on the Day of Judgment

questions.explanation: The Sirat is a bridge stretched over Hell that everyone must cross on the Day of Judgment. Believers will cross with varying speeds; the wicked will fall into Hell.

questions.question: The Islamic greeting 'As-Salamu Alaykum' means:
questions.explanation: 'As-Salamu Alaykum' means 'Peace be upon you.' The complete response is 'Wa Alaykum al-Salam wa Rahmatullahi wa Barakatuh' (And upon you be peace, mercy, and blessings of Allah).

questions.question: The expression 'Bismillah' (In the name of Allah) should be said before:

options.B: beginning any good action such as eating, drinking, or starting work

questions.explanation: 'Bismillah al-Rahman al-Rahim' (In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful) is recommended before all good actions: eating, drinking, reading, beginning any work.

questions.question: The expression 'Alhamdulillah' (All praise is due to Allah) is said:

options.B: when expressing gratitude for any blessing or upon completing an action

questions.explanation: 'Alhamdulillah' (Praise be to Allah) is said as an expression of gratitude for blessings, after good events, upon completing an action, after sneezing, and generally to acknowledge Allah's favours.

questions.question: The expression 'Insha'Allah' means:
questions.explanation: 'Insha'Allah' (If Allah wills) is said when making plans for the future, acknowledging that all future events depend on Allah's will. The Quran commands this in Surah Al-Kahf (18:23-24).

questions.question: Sunan al-Fitra (innate natural practices in Islam) include all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: The Sunan al-Fitra (natural practices) include circumcision, trimming the moustache, trimming nails, shaving pubic hair, removing armpit hair, and using the miswak (tooth stick).

questions.question: Hudud in Islamic law refers to:

options.B: fixed punishments prescribed by the Quran and Sunnah for specific major offences

questions.explanation: Hudud are the fixed punishments prescribed by Allah in the Quran and Sunnah for specific major crimes: theft (amputation of hand), adultery (100 lashes or stoning), false accusation (80 lashes), etc.

questions.question: Qisas in Islamic law means:

options.B: equal retaliation for bodily harm or murder

questions.explanation: Qisas is the principle of equal retaliation under Islamic law — 'an eye for an eye.' Alternatively, the victim or their family can choose to accept Diyya (blood money) or forgive.

questions.question: Diyya in Islamic law is:

options.B: blood money paid as compensation for killing or wounding

questions.explanation: Diyya is financial compensation paid to the victim or their family as an alternative to retaliation (Qisas). The standard Diyya for taking a life is traditionally 100 camels or its monetary equivalent.

questions.question: The Caliphate (Khilafah) in Islamic political thought is:

options.B: the system of Islamic governance representing the succession to the Prophet's (SAW) political leadership

questions.explanation: The Caliphate is the Islamic form of governance where the Caliph (Khalifah) serves as the political successor to the Prophet (SAW) in leading the Muslim community according to Islamic principles.

questions.question: The Treaty of Madinah (Constitution of Madinah), established by the Prophet (SAW), was an agreement between:

options.B: Muslims, Jewish tribes, and other inhabitants of Madinah

questions.explanation: The Constitution of Madinah (c. 622 CE) was a charter drafted by the Prophet (SAW) establishing rights and obligations for the Muslim emigrants, Madinah's Arab tribes, and Jewish communities.

questions.question: Ilm al-Kalam (Islamic theology) is the discipline that deals with:

options.B: rational defense and elaboration of Islamic beliefs using logical arguments

questions.explanation: Ilm al-Kalam (science of discourse/theology) defends Islamic beliefs using rational and philosophical arguments. Major schools include the Ash'ari, Maturidi, and Mu'tazili.

questions.question: Ulumul Quran (the sciences of the Quran) includes the study of:

options.B: everything related to the Quran including revelation, compilation, interpretation, and recitation

questions.explanation: Ulumul Quran encompasses all disciplines related to the Quran: Asbab al-Nuzul, Nasikh wal Mansukh (abrogation), Makki wal Madani Surahs, Tajweed, Tafsir, and i'jaz (inimitability).

questions.question: Nasikh wal Mansukh in Quranic sciences refers to:

options.B: the abrogating and abrogated verses — later verses that supersede earlier ones
options.C: the division of the Quran into parts

questions.explanation: Nasikh (abrogating) refers to later Quranic verses that supersede or modify earlier ones (Mansukh/abrogated). For example, the prohibition of alcohol came gradually through several verses.

questions.question: Tasawwuf (Sufism) in Islam is primarily concerned with:

options.B: the spiritual and inner dimension of Islamic practice — purification of the soul

questions.explanation: Tasawwuf (Sufism) focuses on the inner, spiritual dimension of Islam — purifying the soul (Tazkiyat al-Nafs), developing closeness to Allah, and cultivating noble moral qualities.

questions.question: The concept of Dhikr (remembrance of Allah) is central to Sufi practice. The Quran instructs:

options.C: to remember Allah only in Salah

questions.explanation: The Quran (33:41) commands: 'O you who believe! Remember Allah with much remembrance (Dhikran kathiran).' Dhikr is the constant remembrance of Allah through His names and praises.

questions.question: Islamic civilization in Spain (Al-Andalus) lasted for approximately:
questions.explanation: Islamic rule in Spain (Al-Andalus) lasted approximately 800 years (711-1492 CE), during which Cordoba became one of the greatest centers of learning, art, and science in the world.

questions.question: The Muslim commander who led the conquest of Spain (711 CE) was:
questions.explanation: Tariq ibn Ziyad led the Muslim forces that crossed the Strait of Gibraltar into Spain in 711 CE. The Rock of Gibraltar (Jabal Tariq — Mountain of Tariq) is named after him.

questions.question: Makki Surahs (revealed in Makkah) are generally characterized by:

options.A: detailed legal rulings on family and criminal law
options.B: focus on Tawheed, stories of prophets, the afterlife, and fundamental beliefs
options.C: long verses and comprehensive legal discussions

questions.explanation: Makki Surahs (revealed before Hijrah) typically focus on Tawheed, Iman, stories of prophets, the Day of Judgment, and establishing fundamental beliefs. Madinan Surahs focus on legislation and community building.

questions.question: Madinan Surahs (revealed after Hijrah) are generally characterized by:

options.A: short verses and focus on Tawheed
options.B: detailed legal, social, and political legislation for the Muslim community

questions.explanation: Madinan Surahs (revealed after the Hijrah) focus on Islamic law (marriage, divorce, inheritance, criminal law), social justice, relations with non-Muslims, and consolidating the Muslim community.

questions.question: Which scholar is known as 'Imam al-Muhadditheen' (Imam of Hadith scholars)?
questions.explanation: Imam Muhammad ibn Ismail al-Bukhari is often titled 'Imam al-Muhadditheen' (Imam of Hadith scholars) for his unmatched rigorous standards in authenticating Hadith and compiling Sahih al-Bukhari.

questions.question: Dua (supplication) in Islam is considered:

options.B: the essence of worship — a direct connection between the believer and Allah
options.C: only valid if made in Arabic

questions.explanation: The Prophet (SAW) said: 'Dua is worship (al-Ibadah).' Dua represents the believer's direct communication with Allah at any time, expressing dependence, gratitude, and hope.

questions.question: The eight categories of people entitled to receive Zakah are specified in:

options.C: the Sunnah of the Prophet (SAW) only
options.D: the decisions of the first Caliph

questions.explanation: Surah Al-Tawbah (9:60) specifies the eight categories of Zakah recipients: the poor, the needy, those employed to collect it, those whose hearts need reconciling, freeing captives, debtors, the cause of Allah, and stranded travelers.

questions.question: Zakat al-Fitr (Sadaqat al-Fitr) is obligatory:

options.B: before the Eid al-Fitr prayer, at the end of Ramadan
options.D: annually at the same time as regular Zakah

questions.explanation: Zakat al-Fitr (Fitrah) must be paid before the Eid al-Fitr prayer for every Muslim (including dependants) capable of doing so. It is a food donation (or equivalent) to purify the fast.

questions.question: The Ottoman Empire, which claimed the Islamic Caliphate for centuries, was based in:
questions.explanation: The Ottoman Empire, which lasted from c.1299 to 1922 CE, was based in Istanbul (formerly Constantinople). The Ottomans claimed the Caliphate from the 16th century until its formal abolition in 1924.

questions.question: The Ottoman Sultan who conquered Constantinople (Istanbul) in 1453 CE was:
questions.explanation: Sultan Mehmed II (Mehmed the Conqueror) captured Constantinople from the Byzantine Empire on May 29, 1453 CE, fulfilling a prophecy of the Prophet (SAW) about the city's conquest.

questions.question: The verse 'There is no compulsion in religion' is found in Surah:
questions.explanation: The famous verse 'La ikraha fi al-din' (There is no compulsion in religion, 2:256) is found in Surah Al-Baqarah. It affirms that faith cannot be forced.

questions.question: The verse 'Indeed, with hardship comes ease' (Inna ma'al-usri yusra) is from Surah:
questions.explanation: Surah Al-Inshirah (94:5-6) contains this comforting verse twice: 'Fa inna ma'al-usri yusra, inna ma'al-usri yusra' (So verily, with hardship comes ease, verily with hardship comes ease).

questions.question: The verse on modesty and lowering the gaze is addressed to both men and women in Surah:
questions.explanation: Surah Al-Nur (Chapter 24) contains the verses (24:30-31) instructing both believing men and women to lower their gazes and guard their chastity.

questions.question: The founder of the Tijaniyyah Sufi order, which is widely practiced in Nigeria, was:
questions.explanation: The Tijaniyyah Sufi order was founded by Sheikh Ahmad al-Tijani (1737-1815 CE) in Morocco. It spread widely in West Africa and is one of the most popular Sufi orders in Nigeria.

questions.question: The first Sultan of Sokoto was:
questions.explanation: Usman Dan Fodio (Sheikh Uthman ibn Fudi) was the leader of the Sokoto Jihad and the first commander of the Sokoto Caliphate. His son Muhammad Bello became the first Sultan and administrative head.

questions.question: The Qadiriyyah Sufi order in Nigeria is associated with which prominent leader?
questions.explanation: Usman Dan Fodio and the Sokoto Caliphate are associated with the Qadiriyyah Sufi order (founded by Abd al-Qadir al-Jilani). Dan Fodio later embraced elements of Tijaniyyah.

questions.question: The prophet known for being given the Psalms (Zabur) was:
questions.explanation: Prophet Dawud (David, AS) was given the Zabur (Psalms). He was also a king and was granted by Allah the ability to soften iron and communicate with birds and animals.

questions.question: The prophet whose story of patience and trials is detailed in Surah Yusuf (Joseph) is considered:

options.A: the prophet who spoke to animals
options.B: the most beautiful of prophets and a model of chastity and patience
options.C: the prophet who built the Ark

questions.explanation: Prophet Yusuf (Joseph, AS) is renowned for his extraordinary beauty, patience with his brothers' betrayal, and steadfast chastity when tempted by the wife of Al-Aziz. Surah Yusuf is called 'the best of stories.'

questions.question: Maslahah Mursalah in Islamic jurisprudence refers to:

options.B: consideration of public interest when no specific text addresses an issue

questions.explanation: Maslahah Mursalah (unrestricted public interest) is a secondary legal principle used to derive rulings based on public benefit when no specific Quranic or Sunnah text addresses the issue directly.

questions.question: The year in which the Prophet (SAW) and the early Muslims boycotted in Makkah, enduring severe hardship, is called:
questions.explanation: The Quraysh imposed a social and economic boycott on the Prophet (SAW) and Banu Hashim for approximately three years (616-619 CE), causing severe hardship. This is known as the Year of Boycott.

questions.question: The 'Year of Grief' (Aam al-Huzn) refers to the year in which:

options.B: the Prophet (SAW) lost both his beloved wife Khadijah and uncle Abu Talib
options.C: the Muslims were defeated at Uhud

questions.explanation: The Year of Grief (619 CE/10 BH) was when the Prophet (SAW) lost both his beloved wife Khadijah (RA) and his protective uncle Abu Talib within a short period, weakening his position in Makkah.

questions.question: The first pledge of Aqabah (Bay'at al-Aqabah al-Ula) was made by:

options.B: a group of 12 people from Madinah (Yathrib)

questions.explanation: The First Pledge of Aqabah (621 CE) was made by 12 men from Yathrib (Madinah) who pledged to the Prophet (SAW) to worship only Allah, avoid major sins, and obey him in good matters.

questions.question: The concept of Tawakkul in Islam means:

options.B: complete reliance and trust in Allah while taking the necessary means

questions.explanation: Tawakkul is the Islamic concept of placing complete trust and reliance on Allah while still taking all necessary practical steps. It does not mean passivity but confident action combined with trust in Allah.

questions.question: Khushu in Salah means:

options.B: humility, concentration, and complete presence of heart and mind during prayer

questions.explanation: Khushu is the state of humble concentration, reverence, and presence of heart during Salah — being fully focused on the prayer and aware of standing before Allah. It is the soul of prayer.

questions.question: Maqasid al-Sharia (objectives of Islamic law) are meant to protect which five essential things?

options.A: Faith, life, intellect, lineage, and property
options.B: Prayer, fasting, Zakah, Hajj, and Jihad
options.C: Quran, Sunnah, Ijma, Qiyas, and Ijtihad
options.D: Justice, equality, freedom, democracy, and peace

questions.explanation: Al-Ghazali and later scholars identified five Maqasid (objectives) of Sharia: protection of Deen (faith), Nafs (life), Aql (intellect/mind), Nasl (lineage/progeny), and Mal (property/wealth).

questions.question: The Quran refers to the creation of the universe through a big expansion event, which some relate to the Big Bang, in Surah:
questions.explanation: Surah Al-Anbiya (21:30) states: 'Do the disbelievers not see that the heavens and earth were a joined entity, and We separated them?' This is often discussed in the context of the Big Bang theory.

questions.question: The Battle of Karbala (680 CE), in which Husayn ibn Ali (RA) was martyred, led to the major split between:
questions.explanation: The massacre at Karbala where Husayn ibn Ali (RA) and his companions were killed by Yazid's forces deepened the Sunni-Shia divide that had begun with the dispute over Ali's caliphate.

questions.question: The Crusades were Christian military campaigns primarily aimed at recovering:

options.B: the Holy Land (Jerusalem) from Muslim control

questions.explanation: The Crusades (1095-1291 CE) were a series of religious wars launched by European Christians to recapture the Holy Land (especially Jerusalem) from Muslim control.

questions.question: The Muslim leader who recaptured Jerusalem from the Crusaders in 1187 CE was:
questions.explanation: Saladin (Salah al-Din al-Ayyubi, 1137-1193 CE) defeated the Crusaders at the Battle of Hattin and recaptured Jerusalem in 1187 CE. He was renowned for his chivalry and justice.

questions.question: The concept of Al-Amr bil Ma'ruf wa al-Nahy 'anil Munkar in Islam means:

options.B: commanding what is good and forbidding what is evil

questions.explanation: Al-Amr bil Ma'ruf wa al-Nahy 'anil Munkar (enjoining good and forbidding evil) is a collective duty of the Muslim community. The Quran (3:110) describes this as a characteristic of the best community.
